figures.caption: Table 2: Main results on three multi-hop QA benchmarks. We report EM, F1, ROUGE-L (R-L), Token Cost, and Repair Rate for different agentic RAG baselines with DR-RAG, Rerun, and Step-wise retry. DR-RAG consistently improves answer quality while reducing token consumption across datasets and backbone models. Best results are in bold.

Table 5: Repair success rates stratified by error type and retrieval coverage. Results are broken down by failure category (format, reasoning, search, and retriever errors) and reported separately for full coverage (Cov=1) and partial coverage (Cov<1) across datasets and baselines.

Table 4: Efficiency comparison of different repair strategies. Tok. denotes the average token consumption, and Time represents the total time cost 𝑠 (in seconds). The best results (minimum values) are highlighted in bold.
Figure 3: Accuracy of the automated diagnosis module evaluated against human annotations across different datasets and baselines.

Figure 2: Overview of DR-RAG. Given a failed agentic RAG trajectory, the framework performs taxonomy-constrained diagnosis and localization, followed by tool-conditioned local repair that intervenes at the failure point while reusing validated prefixes.

Figure 5: Case study of a reasoning logic error under full evidence coverage. DR-RAG localizes the earliest faulty reasoning step and repairs the error by reusing retrieved evidence, avoiding unnecessary retrieval and full trajectory regeneration.
Table 6: Oracle-guided upper bound results across datasets. Token cost, Repair Rate, and Improvement are reported.
